title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: reproducible example simulate population: 300 lines, 2000 SNPs, 50 QTL, narrow-sense heritability 0.5.
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 300, m = 2000, n_qtl = 50, h2 = 0.5, seed = 1) dim(sim$geno) #> [1]  300 2000 sim$h2          # realised heritability #> [1] 0.5

title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: Filter low-call-rate, low-MAF monomorphic markers, impute rest.
code: qc <- qc_markers(sim$geno, maf = 0.05, max_missing = 0.1) qc$removed #>   call_rate         maf monomorphic  #>           0          19           0 geno <- qc$geno

what: GBLUP and the genomic relationship matrix
title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: gblup() estimates variance components REML. (GEBVs match rrBLUP::mixed.solve within 6e-5 — see package tests.) geno supplied, fit also carries ridge marker effects, can predict new genotypes:
code: fit <- gblup(sim$pheno, geno) fit #> <gblup> #>   Vu = 50.27, Ve = 37.31, h2 = 0.574 #>   300 individuals; intercept = -0.4076; marker effects available cor(fit$gebv, sim$bv)     # accuracy against the true breeding values #> [1] 0.6941135 train <- 1:240; test <- 241:300 fit_tr <- gs_fit(sim$pheno[train], geno[train, ], model = "gblup") pred   <- predict(fit_tr, geno[test, ]) cor(pred, sim$bv[test]) #> [1] 0.2115857

title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: gs_fit() exposes API every model family. available depends optional packages installed:
code: available_models() #> [1] "gblup"         "elastic_net"   "random_forest" "xgboost"       #> [5] "ensemble" en <- gs_fit(sim$pheno[train], geno[train, ], model = "elastic_net") cor(predict(en, geno[test, ]), sim$bv[test]) #> [1] 0.4801127

title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: gs_cv() runs breeding-relevant cross-validation. Random k-fold predicts untested lines (CV1); leave_group_out holds whole families environments.
code: cv <- gs_cv(sim$pheno, geno, models = "gblup", k = 5, reps = 1, seed = 1) cv #> <gs_cv: kfold> #>   5-fold x 1 rep(s) #>  model  mean    sd n_folds #>  gblup 0.159 0.076       5 #>   (accuracy = predictive ability, cor(pred, observed) on held-out data)  # leave-one-family-out, with a toy family structure fam <- rep(1:6, length.out = nrow(geno)) gs_cv(sim$pheno, geno, models = "gblup",       scheme = "leave_group_out", groups = fam) #> <gs_cv: leave_group_out> #>  model  mean    sd n_folds #>  gblup 0.196 0.166       6 #>   (accuracy = predictive ability, cor(pred, observed) on held-out data)

title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: gs_ensemble() combines base models super-learner: model’s --fold predictions used learn non-negative weights (summing one), final predictor weighted blend. several base models installed, weights show much contributes.
code: ens <- gs_ensemble(sim$pheno, geno, base_models = "gblup", seed = 1) ens$weights #> gblup  #>     1

title: Benchmarking genomic prediction models with GSbench
text: gs_benchmark() runs every available model — including ensemble — one cross-validation reports predictive ability can compare fairly.  additive trait like one, GBLUP elastic net typically strongest; tree methods trail; ensemble lands near top without pick winner advance.
code: bench <- gs_benchmark(sim$pheno, geno, models = c("gblup", "ensemble"),                       k = 5, seed = 1) bench$accuracy #>      model      mean         sd n_folds #> 1 ensemble 0.2669233 0.06176019       5 #> 2    gblup 0.1590768 0.07638873       5 plot(bench)

title: Authors and Citation
text: Muhammad Farooqi. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Farooqi M (2026). GSbench: Benchmarking Genomic Selection Machine-Learning Prediction Models. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/mqfarooqi1/GSbench.
code: @Manual{,   title = {{GSbench}: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models},   author = {Muhammad Farooqi},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/mqfarooqi1/GSbench}, }

what: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
text: Benchmark genomic-selection models — classic machine-learning — SNP marker data, one interface, breeding-relevant cross-validation honest accuracy reporting. problem GSbench addresses: people increasingly throw glmnet, ranger, xgboost marker matrices, hand-roll cross-validation (often incorrectly) compare models unequal footing. GSbench fits standard baselines (GBLUP, ridge marker effects) ML methods behind single gs_fit()/predict() API, runs CV, reports predictive ability can actually trust — plus stacked ensemble combines .

title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
text: GSbench CRAN (published 2026-06-30): graphics, stats withr required. ML backends — glmnet, ranger, xgboost — optional (Suggests); install whichever want use.
code: # from CRAN (recommended) install.packages("GSbench")  # or from R-universe install.packages("GSbench", repos = "https://mqfarooqi1.r-universe.dev")  # or the development version from GitHub # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("mqfarooqi1/GSbench")

title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
code: library(GSbench)  sim <- simulate_population(n = 300, m = 2000, h2 = 0.5, seed = 1)  # one model fit <- gs_fit(sim$pheno, sim$geno, model = "gblup") gebv <- predict(fit, sim$geno)  # compare every available model (incl. the stacked ensemble) under one CV bench <- gs_benchmark(sim$pheno, sim$geno, k = 5, seed = 1) bench plot(bench) model  mean    sd n_folds    elastic_net 0.367 0.187       5          gblup 0.334 0.189       5       ensemble 0.328 0.165       5  random_forest 0.269 0.185       5        xgboost 0.185 0.318       5   (accuracy = predictive ability, cor(pred, observed) on held-out data)

title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
text: Core (base R, compiled code, heavy deps): Modelling & evaluation:

title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
text: GBLUP built scratch base R (spectral REML, Endelman 2011 / EMMA method) numerically validated rrBLUP test suite — variance components, GEBVs correlating 1.0. Cross-validation part people get wrong, ’s part GSbench opinionated : correct fold construction, leave-group-family/environment structure, accuracy aggregated across folds. stacked ensemble Breiman / van der Laan super-learner: base models combined weights fit --fold predictions (non-negative, summing one). tends match beat best single model without know advance.

title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
text: Single trait, single environment. Multi-trait GxE (CV2) models yet — ’s obvious next direction. Pure-R performance. GBLUP solver eigendecomposes n×n matrix; fine typical breeding populations (hundreds–thousand lines), large panels want C++ backend. Imputation simple (marker means); model-based imputation upstream better real data. simulator demos/tests — bring genotypes phenotypes real work.

title: Benchmarking Genomic Selection and Machine-Learning Prediction Models
text: VanRaden, P. M. (2008) J. Dairy Sci. 91:4414–4423. doi:10.3168/jds.2007-0980 Endelman, J. B. (2011) Plant Genome 4:250–255. doi:10.3835/plantgenome2011.08.0024 Meuwissen, Hayes & Goddard (2001) Genetics 157:1819–1829. doi:10.1093/genetics/157.4.1819 van der Laan, Polley & Hubbard (2007) Stat. Appl. Genet. Mol. Biol. 6:Art.25. doi:10.2202/1544-6115.1309 Muhammad Farooqi · https://github.com/mqfarooqi1

title: Dominance genomic relationship matrix — Dmatrix
text: Builds dominance genomic relationship matrix allele dosages using genotypic parameterisation Vitezica et al. (2013): homozygotes heterozygotes coded matrix captures dominance (heterozygote versus homozygote) deviations, complementing additive relationships Gmatrix(). Supplying gblup() K fits dominance GBLUP model, additive dominance GEBVs can compared.

title: Dominance genomic relationship matrix — Dmatrix
code: Dmatrix(geno, min_maf = 0)

title: Dominance genomic relationship matrix — Dmatrix
text: geno numeric marker matrix (individuals x markers), coded 0/1/2, missing values (run qc_markers() impute_markers() first). min_maf Markers minor allele frequency dropped building matrix. Default 0 (keep supplied markers).

title: Dominance genomic relationship matrix — Dmatrix
text: n x n symmetric dominance relationship matrix, row/column names taken rownames(geno).

title: Dominance genomic relationship matrix — Dmatrix
text: Vitezica, Z. G., Varona, L. & Legarra, . (2013) "additive dominant variance covariance individuals within genomic selection scope." Genetics 195, 1223-1230. doi:10.1534/genetics.113.155176

title: Dominance genomic relationship matrix — Dmatrix
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 80, m = 400, seed = 1) D <- Dmatrix(sim$geno) dim(D) #> [1] 80 80 # dominance GBLUP: fit <- gblup(sim$pheno, K = D)

what: GSbench: benchmarking genomic selection and machine-learning models — GSbench-package
title: GSbench: benchmarking genomic selection and machine-learning models — GSbench-package
text: GSbench provides single, consistent interface genomic prediction SNP markers. implements GBLUP ridge-regression BLUP base R, wraps machine-learning predictors (elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting) behind interface, adds stacked ensemble. Models compared breeding-relevant cross-validation honest accuracy reporting.

title: GSbench: benchmarking genomic selection and machine-learning models — GSbench-package
text: simulate_population() makes toy dataset, gblup() fits GBLUP baseline, (later sections package) cross-validation benchmarking functions compare models.

title: GSbench: benchmarking genomic selection and machine-learning models — GSbench-package
text: package uses S3 object system. Core numerical methods (genomic relationship matrix mixed-model solver) written base R compiled code, package installs without toolchain. Machine-learning backends optional (declared Suggests) used installed.

title: GSbench: benchmarking genomic selection and machine-learning models — GSbench-package
text: Maintainer: Muhammad Farooqi mqfarooqi@gmail.com Authors: Muhammad Farooqi mqfarooqi@gmail.com

title: Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) — Gmatrix
text: Builds additive genomic relationship matrix allele dosages using VanRaden's first method: markers centred twice allele frequency, G = W W' / (2 * sum(p (1 - p))). standard additive GRM used GBLUP.

title: Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) — Gmatrix
code: Gmatrix(geno, min_maf = 0)

title: Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) — Gmatrix
text: geno numeric marker matrix (individuals x markers), coded 0/1/2, missing values (run qc_markers() impute_markers() first). min_maf Markers minor allele frequency dropped building matrix. Default 0 (keep supplied markers).

title: Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) — Gmatrix
text: n x n symmetric genomic relationship matrix row/column names taken rownames(geno).

title: Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) — Gmatrix
text: VanRaden, P. M. (2008) "Efficient methods compute genomic predictions." Journal Dairy Science 91, 4414-4423. doi:10.3168/jds.2007-0980

title: Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) — Gmatrix
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 80, m = 400, seed = 1) G <- Gmatrix(sim$geno) dim(G) #> [1] 80 80

what: Models available in this session — available_models
title: Models available in this session — available_models
text: Returns genomic-prediction models GSbench can currently run. "gblup" always available; machine-learning models require (suggested) package installed.

title: Models available in this session — available_models
code: available_models()

title: Models available in this session — available_models
text: character vector usable model names.

title: Models available in this session — available_models
code: available_models() #> [1] "gblup"         "elastic_net"   "random_forest" "xgboost"       #> [5] "ensemble"

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
text: Fits genomic best linear unbiased prediction model y = X b + g + e, g ~ N(0, G s2u) e ~ N(0, s2e), estimating variance components restricted maximum likelihood (REML). solver uses spectral / eigendecomposition approach Endelman (2011) (method rrBLUP::mixed.solve): REML log-likelihood profiled one-dimensional optimisation variance ratio lambda = s2e / s2u.

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
code: gblup(y, geno = NULL, K = NULL, X = NULL)

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
text: y Numeric phenotype vector (length n), missing values. geno Optional marker matrix (n x m, coded 0/1/2). Used build K derive marker effects --sample prediction. K Optional n x n genomic relationship matrix. NULL, built geno. pass K directly (geno), --sample prediction available. X Optional fixed-effects design matrix (n x p). Defaults intercept.

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
text: object class gblup: list Vu, Ve, h2, beta (fixed effects), gebv (length-n GEBVs), lambda, K, — geno given — marker_effects, marker_means (2p) intercept.

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
text: geno supplied, equivalent ridge-regression marker effects also returned, fit can predict breeding values new genotypes via predict.gblup().

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
text: Endelman, J. B. (2011) "Ridge regression kernels genomic selection R package rrBLUP." Plant Genome 4, 250-255. doi:10.3835/plantgenome2011.08.0024

title: Fit GBLUP by REML — gblup
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 120, m = 500, seed = 1) fit <- gblup(sim$pheno, sim$geno) fit$h2 #> [1] 0.6814213 cor(fit$gebv, sim$bv) #> [1] 0.773269

what: Benchmark all available genomic prediction models — gs_benchmark
title: Benchmark all available genomic prediction models — gs_benchmark
text: Convenience wrapper around gs_cv() evaluates every model available session (including stacked "ensemble") one cross-validation, can compared equal footing. Returns gs_cv object; use plot() draw comparison.

title: Benchmark all available genomic prediction models — gs_benchmark
code: gs_benchmark(   y,   geno,   models = available_models(),   k = 5,   reps = 1,   scheme = c("kfold", "leave_group_out"),   groups = NULL,   seed = NULL,   ... )

title: Benchmark all available genomic prediction models — gs_benchmark
text: y Numeric phenotype vector (length n), missing values. geno Marker matrix (n x m, 0/1/2, missing values). models Models evaluate; defaults available (see available_models()). Unavailable models dropped warning. k Number folds "kfold". Default 5. reps Number repeats "kfold". Default 1. scheme "kfold" "leave_group_out". groups Grouping vector (length n) "leave_group_out". seed Optional seed (applied via withr::with_seed()). ... Passed gs_fit() (model hyperparameters).

title: Benchmark all available genomic prediction models — gs_benchmark
text: gs_cv object (see gs_cv()).

title: Benchmark all available genomic prediction models — gs_benchmark
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 120, m = 400, seed = 1) bench <- gs_benchmark(sim$pheno, sim$geno, models = "gblup", k = 5, seed = 1) bench$accuracy #>   model      mean        sd n_folds #> 1 gblup 0.1530038 0.1057235       5

title: Cross-validate genomic prediction models — gs_cv
text: Runs breeding-relevant cross-validation reports predictive ability (correlation predictions observed phenotypes held-individuals) model. Two schemes supported: "kfold" (random k-fold, .e. predicting untested lines, repeated reps times) "leave_group_out" (hold one family/environment time, via groups).

title: Cross-validate genomic prediction models — gs_cv
code: gs_cv(   y,   geno,   models = available_models(),   k = 5,   reps = 1,   scheme = c("kfold", "leave_group_out"),   groups = NULL,   seed = NULL,   ... )

title: Cross-validate genomic prediction models — gs_cv
text: y Numeric phenotype vector (length n), missing values. geno Marker matrix (n x m, 0/1/2, missing values). models Models evaluate; defaults available (see available_models()). Unavailable models dropped warning. k Number folds "kfold". Default 5. reps Number repeats "kfold". Default 1. scheme "kfold" "leave_group_out". groups Grouping vector (length n) "leave_group_out". seed Optional seed (applied via withr::with_seed()). ... Passed gs_fit() (model hyperparameters).

title: Cross-validate genomic prediction models — gs_cv
text: object class gs_cv: list accuracy (data frame model, mean, sd, n_folds), per_fold (raw fold results), call settings.

title: Cross-validate genomic prediction models — gs_cv
text: Fitting wrapped model errors fold records NA fold rather aborting whole run.

title: Cross-validate genomic prediction models — gs_cv
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 120, m = 400, seed = 1) cv <- gs_cv(sim$pheno, sim$geno, models = "gblup", k = 5, seed = 1) cv #> <gs_cv: kfold> #>   5-fold x 1 rep(s) #>  model  mean    sd n_folds #>  gblup 0.153 0.106       5 #>   (accuracy = predictive ability, cor(pred, observed) on held-out data)

what: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
title: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
text: Combines several base models one predictor stacking: base model's --fold cross-validated predictions used learn set non-negative weights (constrained sum one), final prediction weighted average base models refit data. Breiman / van der Laan stacked-regression (super-learner) idea applied genomic selection; practice tends match beat best single model without know advance .

title: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
code: gs_ensemble(y, geno, base_models = NULL, inner_k = 5, seed = NULL, ...)

title: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
text: y Numeric phenotype vector (length n), missing values. geno Marker matrix (n x m, 0/1/2, missing values). base_models Character vector base model names. Defaults every available model except ensemble . inner_k Folds inner stacking cross-validation. Default 5. seed Optional seed inner folds (via withr::with_seed()). ... Passed gs_fit() base models.

title: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
text: object class gs_ensemble (gs_model): list base_names, weights (named, summing 1), refit base_fits, --fold prediction matrix oof.

title: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
text: van der Laan, M. J., Polley, E. C. Hubbard, . E. (2007) "Super Learner." Statistical Applications Genetics Molecular Biology 6, Article 25. doi:10.2202/1544-6115.1309

title: Stacked super-learner ensemble of genomic prediction models — gs_ensemble
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 100, m = 300, seed = 1) ens <- gs_ensemble(sim$pheno, sim$geno, base_models = "gblup", seed = 1) ens$weights #> gblup  #>     1

what: Fit a genomic prediction model — gs_fit
title: Fit a genomic prediction model — gs_fit
text: One interface several model families: "gblup" (always available), "elastic_net" (glmnet), "random_forest" (ranger), "xgboost" (xgboost), "ensemble" (stacked super-learner; see gs_ensemble()). returned object predict() method takes new marker matrix.

title: Fit a genomic prediction model — gs_fit
code: gs_fit(y, geno, model = "gblup", ...)

title: Fit a genomic prediction model — gs_fit
text: y Numeric phenotype vector (length n), missing values. geno Marker matrix (n x m, coded 0/1/2, missing values). model Model name; see available_models(). ... Model-specific hyperparameters (e.g. alpha elastic net, num.trees random forest, nrounds/eta/max_depth xgboost, base_models ensemble).

title: Fit a genomic prediction model — gs_fit
text: object class gs_model wrapping fitted model.

title: Fit a genomic prediction model — gs_fit
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 120, m = 400, seed = 1) fit <- gs_fit(sim$pheno, sim$geno, model = "gblup") head(predict(fit, sim$geno)) #> [1] -3.8752143 -5.4217031  0.7654262  6.0236117  5.7933099 -4.5519275

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
text: Tests marker additive association phenotype, optionally correcting population structure genotype principal components. complements whole-genome prediction models GSbench: prediction asks well can predict, whereas GWAS asks markers associated.

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
code: gwas(y, geno, n_pc = 0, covariates = NULL, map = NULL, min_maf = 0)  # S3 method for class 'gs_gwas' print(x, top = 6, ...)

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
text: y Numeric phenotype vector (length n). Missing values dropped. geno Marker matrix (n x m), coded 0/1/2, missing values. n_pc Number genotype principal components include covariates structure correction (default 0). covariates Optional numeric matrix additional fixed covariates (n x k). map Optional data frame per-marker annotation; columns chrom pos (present) carried enable chromosome-aware Manhattan plots. min_maf Drop markers minor-allele frequency testing (default 0). x gs_gwas object. top Number top associations show (default 6). ... arguments passed plotting functions.

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
text: object class gs_gwas (data frame) one row per marker: marker, optionally chrom pos, effect, se, statistic, p_value log10p. print() plot() methods (Manhattan QQ plots); see plot.gs_gwas().

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
text: speed phenotype markers projected onto residual space covariates (intercept, optional principal components extra covariates), partial regression slope marker tested t-test. algebraically equivalent fitting one linear model per marker avoids per-marker loop.

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
text: print(gs_gwas): Print short summary top associations.

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
text: Yu, J. et al. (2006) "unified mixed-model method association mapping accounts multiple levels relatedness." Nature Genetics 38, 203-208. doi:10.1038/ng1702

title: Single-marker genome-wide association scan — gwas
Impute missing marker genotypes — impute_markers
text: Replaces missing values marker (column) marker's mean dosage (.e. twice estimated allele frequency). Simple fast; production work model-based imputation upstream preferable.

title: Impute missing marker genotypes — impute_markers
code: impute_markers(geno)

title: Impute missing marker genotypes — impute_markers
text: geno numeric marker matrix (individuals x markers), 0/1/2, possibly NAs.

title: Impute missing marker genotypes — impute_markers
text: matrix NAs filled column means. Columns entirely missing filled 0.

title: Impute missing marker genotypes — impute_markers
Plot a model comparison — plot.gs_cv
text: Bar chart mean predictive ability per model, +/- 1 SD whiskers across folds.

title: Plot a model comparison — plot.gs_cv
code: # S3 method for class 'gs_cv' plot(x, ...)

title: Plot a model comparison — plot.gs_cv
text: x gs_cv object. ... Passed graphics::barplot().

title: Plot a model comparison — plot.gs_cv
text: x, invisibly.

title: Plot a model comparison — plot.gs_cv
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 120, m = 400, seed = 1) # \donttest{ plot(gs_cv(sim$pheno, sim$geno, models = "gblup", k = 5, seed = 1))  # }

what: Plot a GWAS scan (Manhattan or QQ) — plot.gs_gwas
title: Plot a GWAS scan (Manhattan or QQ) — plot.gs_gwas
text: Plot GWAS scan (Manhattan QQ)

title: Plot a GWAS scan (Manhattan or QQ) — plot.gs_gwas
code: # S3 method for class 'gs_gwas' plot(x, type = c("manhattan", "qq"), alpha = 0.05, ...)

title: Plot a GWAS scan (Manhattan or QQ) — plot.gs_gwas
text: x gs_gwas object gwas(). type "manhattan" (default) "qq". alpha Genome-wide significance level Bonferroni line Manhattan plot (default 0.05). ... graphical parameters passed graphics::plot().

title: Plot a GWAS scan (Manhattan or QQ) — plot.gs_gwas
text: gs_gwas object, invisibly.

title: Plot a GWAS scan (Manhattan or QQ) — plot.gs_gwas
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 150, m = 500, n_qtl = 10, h2 = 0.6, seed = 1) scan <- gwas(sim$pheno, sim$geno, n_pc = 3) plot(scan)  plot(scan, type = "qq")

what: Predict breeding values for new genotypes from a GBLUP fit — predict.gblup

what: Quality-control filter for marker data — qc_markers
title: Quality-control filter for marker data — qc_markers
text: Drops markers failing call-rate minor-allele-frequency threshold, (optionally) monomorphic markers, imputes remaining missing values.

title: Quality-control filter for marker data — qc_markers
code: qc_markers(geno, maf = 0.05, max_missing = 0.1, impute = TRUE)

title: Quality-control filter for marker data — qc_markers
text: geno numeric marker matrix (individuals x markers), coded 0/1/2. maf Minimum minor allele frequency keep marker. Default 0.05. max_missing Maximum fraction missing calls keep marker. Default 0.1. impute Whether mean-impute remaining missing values. Default TRUE.

title: Quality-control filter for marker data — qc_markers
text: list geno (filtered, optionally imputed matrix) removed (named integer vector counting markers dropped rule).

title: Quality-control filter for marker data — qc_markers
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 50, m = 200, seed = 1) qc <- qc_markers(sim$geno) dim(qc$geno) #> [1]  50 199 qc$removed #>   call_rate         maf monomorphic  #>           0           1           0

what: Simulate a genomic prediction dataset — simulate_population
title: Simulate a genomic prediction dataset — simulate_population
text: Generates small marker matrix phenotype known narrow-sense heritability, examples, tests demonstrations. Markers coded allele dosages (0, 1, 2). random subset markers QTL additive effects; phenotype resulting breeding value plus normal noise scaled target heritability.

title: Simulate a genomic prediction dataset — simulate_population
code: simulate_population(n = 200, m = 1000, n_qtl = 50, h2 = 0.5, seed = NULL)

title: Simulate a genomic prediction dataset — simulate_population
text: n Number individuals. Default 200. m Number markers. Default 1000. n_qtl Number markers non-zero (QTL) effect. Default 50. h2 Target narrow-sense heritability (0, 1]. Default 0.5. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility. Applied withr::with_seed(), scopes seed locally leaves caller's random-number state unchanged.

title: Simulate a genomic prediction dataset — simulate_population
text: list geno (n x m 0/1/2 matrix, row/column names set), pheno (length-n numeric), bv (true breeding values), qtl (indices causal markers) h2 (realised heritability).

title: Simulate a genomic prediction dataset — simulate_population
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 100, m = 300, seed = 1) dim(sim$geno) #> [1] 100 300 length(sim$pheno) #> [1] 100

what: Summary of a cross-validation / benchmark — summary.gs_cv
title: Summary of a cross-validation / benchmark — summary.gs_cv
text: Summary cross-validation / benchmark

title: Summary of a cross-validation / benchmark — summary.gs_cv
code: # S3 method for class 'gs_cv' summary(object, ...)

title: Summary of a cross-validation / benchmark — summary.gs_cv
text: object gs_cv object. ... Ignored.

title: Summary of a cross-validation / benchmark — summary.gs_cv
text: accuracy data frame, invisibly.

title: Summary of a cross-validation / benchmark — summary.gs_cv
code: sim <- simulate_population(n = 100, m = 300, seed = 1) summary(gs_cv(sim$pheno, sim$geno, models = "gblup", seed = 1)) #> <gs_cv: kfold> #>   5-fold x 1 rep(s) #>  model mean    sd n_folds #>  gblup 0.11 0.276       5 #>   (accuracy = predictive ability, cor(pred, observed) on held-out data)

title: GSbench 0.2.0
text: New features: gwas() performs single-marker genome-wide association scan optional principal-component structure correction, returning gs_gwas object print() plot() methods (Manhattan QQ plots). Dmatrix() builds dominance genomic relationship matrix (Vitezica et al. 2013), can passed gblup() K fit dominance GBLUP model.

title: GSbench 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-06-30 First release. Core (base R): simulate_population(), qc_markers(), impute_markers(), Gmatrix() (VanRaden), gblup() (GBLUP REML, validated rrBLUP::mixed.solve). Unified modelling interface gs_fit() / predict() covering GBLUP, elastic net (glmnet), random forest (ranger) gradient boosting (xgboost). gs_cv() breeding-relevant cross-validation (random k-fold leave-one-group-). gs_ensemble(), stacked super-learner combining base models non-negative, --fold-fitted weights. gs_benchmark() print/summary/plot compare available models one cross-validation.
